column7: message

>America needs an efficient healthcare system for both moral and economic reasons. Repealing Obamacare without a viable replacement, that address and improves on systemic inefficiencies, is bad for America

But what is that system? We only see single payer working in small all white countries or very rich oil juntas or in countries where the state of medicine is rather primitive so racking up millions of dollars on one individual is impossible. End of life care is one of the big drivers of healthcare cost. Trying to squeeze out a few more months, a few more days, a few more hours can be staggeringly expensive when your tool box is as big as the modern American healthcare systems.

I am not opposed to single payer in theory. There are places in the world where it works on both a financial and moral level, but America simply isn't one of those places.

What in the world are you talking about? Small, all white, countries? 1 wtf does "all white" have to do with anything? 2 the US is one of only a few first world countries *not* to have single payer healthcare. The vast majority of first world countries do have single payer and aren't going bankrupt.

@frigid-dimension @syrupy-bridge So, we're saying that because some countries manage to provide healthcare efficiently, the U.S. can't? Sounds like we're just too special and complex for that simple solution. And @syrupy-bridge, "all white" was just pointing out demographics, not implying anything else. Let's keep it chill, okay?
"@pale-property @syrupy-bridge Let's focus on the policy discussion and avoid assumptions about each other's intentions. Keep the dialogue respectful and constructive."

column7: Clearly you're taking my metaphor way too seriously. Imagine a circumstance where a group of people could have affected something bad from happening and chose not to and rather showed support instead. showing support is patriotic showing support is a marketing scheme because showing support means nothing if the one thing that you're showing support for is failing. Like the government or military or your kid with cancer. Based off of your response I have a feeling that your anti socialism.

If my kid had cancer I would absolutely not expect people to help me pay the bills, that's my responsibility. Saying they would pray for my child is a show of support, the fact that it won't have a direct impact on his recovery is irrelevant. I don't think anyone I know is capable of curing cancer.

So what is the base level of support for being patriotic? Is it purely financial? If so would you acknowledge that someone can be patriotic if they are paying their taxes as expected?

I would argue that it absolutely depends on the individual, different people are capable of different levels of support. Just as I wouldn't expect anyone I know to cure cancer, I likewise wouldn't expect anyone I know to be able to fix any of the country's issues.

@largo-buck So praying fixes everything now? Sounds like some pretty efficient support there. And yeah, paying taxes = patriotism, got it. Next you'll tell me believing in Santa also makes me a great citizen.

column7:
I like your strawman argument of the NRA. It was quite funny.

I noticed you only talked about how guns kill people. But what about how many times every year guns have saved people's lives. You haven't considered that legislation that affects gun ownership could also have a negative impact on guns saving lives. Do you know how many times every year that guns are used defensively to protect people?
Anything with an ejectable mag can house extended mags. this isn't exclusive to the ARs and you can find a large variety of semi auto hunting rifles. The most obvious being the Ruger Mini14

It seems like we're focusing on different aspects of the issue. While defensive uses are important, we can't ignore the frequency of gun-related tragedies. Violet-stack, you bring up a good point about other semi-auto rifles, but the debate often centers around AR-15s due to their prevalence in mass shootings. Let's keep it constructive, folks.

column7:
So, your first point, I totally agree with. Governments can be overthrown, and as to your third point, You're right that the "prevented" opinions will spread in other ways, see

>People who are silenced always speak louder than their silencer, meaning that ideas will spread quicker, and likely "underground". Limiting free speech will inherently bring a new free thought renaissance.

as well as

> I've seen absolutely no evidence in any arguments supporting these that they curb the tide of hate speech. Hell, even in Europe where hate speech laws are rampant, identitarian movements and other hate-based beliefs are growing exponentially.

on my post.

It's the second point that concerns me. To me, you've sort of just graphed out a reason why hate speech laws don't need to exist: Because society's aversion to taboos and profanities does it for us.

>Giving away any right to the government, for any reason, cannot be taken back. Imagine if we handed away the fourth amendment. The cops could barge in any old time without reason.

>Giving the right to silence permanently opens the door to government-sponsored censorship.

The government is not some kind of divinely protected entity. There is a reason rebellion occurs and why some of those in powerful positions may happen to allow it. If there is sufficient demand to organize one and have insider support then governments are at risk.

>You cannot get rid of hate speech laws once you have them. After all, wouldn't removing them be siding with hate speech?

This is entirely reinforced by socially ingrained value appeal. In some cases these socially ingrained values have changed over time while in others they have not. There are all kinds of ways someone can slander another regardless as long as they have a significant enough voice to say something against, as we should know from the behavior of SJWs and the alt-right towards each other today and everyone that buys anything one side says about the other.

>What I'm trying to say is, if society is never exposed to differing arguments, said differing arguments will spread like the plague.

This doesn't make sense. I know what you mean, but the logic does not follow. Society *is* going to be exposed to differing arguments, its just that they will employ different means to do so. Even with free speech currently in effect the same things you are mentioning are happening right now, because the government doesn't even have to do this job for you, social standards in general reduce people to taking it to where they think they can get away with it.

I think the key here is understanding that societal norms play a huge role in curbing hate speech. Laws might seem like a quick fix, but they can backfire and create underground movements. Plus, history shows that once a government has power, it’s hard to take it back.

column7: So... having a working model of the world which includes the possibility of improbable experiences is bad?  How could you ever update your model, then?  Once it's established, anything new you encounter will seem improbable.

I legitimately don't understand what is inconsistent about the religious viewpoint you describe.  Are you saying that having an IMPROBABLE experience means your worldview must be inconsistent somehow?

It’s not about the improbability itself, but how one interprets and integrates those experiences into their worldview without disregarding evidence or rationality. We can update our models, sure, but let’s do it based on solid grounds, not just because something feels right or happened once.
